summary: Jeff Atwood delivers a scathing critique of Microsoft's GotDotNet developer community site, documenting its persistent reliability, performance, and usability failures. He argues that the site's poor quality reflects badly on the entire .NET ecosystem due to its official Microsoft association. After listing numerous failings and unfavorably comparing it to SourceForge, he calls for Microsoft to either fix it properly or shut it down entirely. The post ends with an unexpected twist: a revamped ASP.NET 2.0 version launched that same day, addressing his main complaints about speed and design.
key_points: GotDotNet suffered from chronic unreliability, poor performance, bad design, and limited functionality for over three years
The site's official Microsoft branding meant its failures damaged the reputation of the entire .NET platform
Even the mediocre SourceForge provided a better user experience than this Microsoft-sponsored alternative
Microsoft's failure to improve or kill underperforming products sends a negative signal to the developer community
The newly released ASP.NET 2.0 version finally addressed the core speed and design issues
money_quotes: GotDotNet is an embarrassing trainwreck of epic proportions.
Why is it that fully half of my interactions with GotDotNet are extremely unpleasant?
When you can't muster a user experience comparable to the thoroughly mediocre SourceForge, it's time to seriously reconsider your reason for existing at all.
GotDotNet drags down the entire .NET ecosystem. And that's a damn shame.
Either have the guts to kill GotDotNet outright, or put your alpha development triage team on it ASAP.
At least using it is no longer like randomly jabbing myself in the face with punji sticks.
key_insight: Developer tools and platforms carrying a major company's branding must meet higher standards because their failures reflect on the entire ecosystem they represent.
